I have a set of rules that are a little bit helpful here. I'll start with that. When I call the 4% rule, it's supposed that you're going to retire and you want enough to last you from your capital throughout the rest of your life.
I would say it's a pretty good working rule. It's put mostly in equities and spend 4% of your capital each year or less if you can.
And that ought to last you from say the 60s until the end of your life. It's not guaranteed, but pretty good chance it will.
Then I have the 2% rule, which I've found by studies both mathematical and by simulation of stock returns.
If you only drain 2% out per year, then that money will probably grow in perpetuity.
